attributes.stem:
A 78-year-old woman is admitted to hospital with community-acquired pneumonia complicated by a pleural effusion requiring a chest drain. She has a medical history of stage three chronic kidney disease, heart failure with reduced ejection fraction secondary to hypertensive disease, stable angina and type 2 diabetes.

While on the ward, she falls on the way to the bathroom and sustains a right proximal humerus fracture, which will require surgery. Prior to her operation, the acute pain service recommends regional anaesthesia and performs an interscalene block using bupivacaine. Shortly after the procedure, the patient complains of a strange sensation around her mouth and becomes notably agitated before suddenly convulsing. On examination, she appears unwell. Her pulse rate is 120/min, blood pressure 160/90 mmHg, respiratory rate 24/min, SpO2 94% on room air, and temperature 36.8°C.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is an early indicator of the condition this patient is experiencing?
attributes.explanation:
This patient is experiencing local anaesthetic systemic toxicity (LAST), a life-threatening complication of local anaesthetic administration. LAST is usually caused by either inadvertent intravascular injection or a dosage error. The patient's age, renal impairment, and cardiac disease are risk factors for LAST.

The symptoms and signs of LAST occur in a biphasic pattern. Initially, there is a depression of excitatory neurons resulting in early signs and symptoms, followed by a depression of inhibitory neurons resulting in late signs and symptoms. These can be categorised into those affecting the central nervous system and those affecting the cardiovascular system.

Tinnitus is one of the early signs of LAST affecting the central nervous system. Other early signs include agitation (as seen in this patient), confusion, dizziness, drowsiness, dysphoria, perioral numbness, and a metallic taste. The patient's complaint of a strange sensation around her mouth likely represents perioral numbness, another early sign of LAST.

Respiratory depression is a late clinical sign of LAST due to the depression of inhibitory neurons and is associated with severe toxicity. It would not be expected as an early indicator.

Bradyarrhythmia is a late cardiovascular sign of LAST, occurring after the initial hypertension and tachycardia (which are present in this case). Bradyarrhythmias occur due to the depression of inhibitory neurons and are associated with severe toxicity.

Right unilateral ptosis is not a sign of LAST. It may occur as a complication of an interscalene block if the sympathetic chain is affected (Horner's syndrome). However, this local effect does not explain the systemic symptoms presented in the stem.

Hoarse voice is another potential complication of an interscalene block due to involvement of the recurrent laryngeal nerve. Like ptosis, it is a local effect of the block rather than a sign of systemic toxicity and is inconsistent with the other systemic symptoms described.

The immediate treatment of LAST includes cessation of the local anaesthetic and calling for help. Depending on the severity, management may also involve ventilatory support, cardiovascular support, and seizure management. The definitive treatment is intravenous lipid emulsion therapy.
learningPoints: Local anaesthetic systemic toxicity (LAST) is a severe and life-threatening adverse effect of local anaesthetics.
Early signs of local anaesthetic systemic toxicity include agitation, confusion, dizziness, drowsiness, dysphoria, tinnitus and perioral numbness.
references: Christie LE, Picard J, Weinberg GL. Local anaesthetic systemic toxicity. BJA education. 2015;15(3):136–42. Available at: https://academic.oup.com/bjaed/article/15/3/136/279390
El-Boghdadly K, Pawa A, Chin KJ. Local anesthetic systemic toxicity: current perspectives. Local and Regional Anesthesia. 2018;11:35–44. Available at: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/30122981/
